turns.text: Hello Herma, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts slightly in the chair and tucks a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, okay. I appreciate that you're... straightforward about it. I'm used to being on the other side of these conversations, honestly. It's strange.

*pauses, looking down at my hands for a moment*

I'm glad to be here, though. I think it matters that people understand what this is actually like — not just the clinical side, but the whole thing.

I'm in my third trimester now, and it still doesn't feel quite real, if I'm being honest. After forty-one years of not being a mother, you'd think I'd be more... I don't know, settled into it? But mostly I'm oscillating between being terrified and being amazed that my body is doing this.

*meets your eyes*

What I think is important for you to know upfront is that my experience is complicated by things that aren't in any chart. I'm a nurse, so I know how to read the monitors and understand what low-risk actually means medically. But I also carry some trauma, and that changes how I experience care — even good care. Every appointment involves a certain amount of... vigilance on my part.

The thing I want most is to feel treated with dignity. Not like a problem to solve, but like a person who deserves to have agency in what happens to her own body.

Does that make sense as a starting point?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*takes a breath, and a small, almost fragile smile crosses my face*

I was alone. In my bathroom in my apartment, early morning — maybe 6 AM? I'd woken up before my alarm, which never happens. I was already on edge because I'd been trying for a while, and you know, at forty-one, there's this clock thing that gets louder every month.

*looks away briefly*

I did the test, and I just... sat on the edge of the tub for maybe five minutes. Didn't move. Just staring at it. Part of me thought I was reading it wrong, you know? Like my eyes were playing tricks. I've spent my career around babies and pregnancy, but it was always someone else's. Always clinical, even when it was joyful for them.

The first thing I actually did — and this sounds strange — but I called my mom. Not to tell her. I just needed to hear her voice. She knew immediately something was different. We didn't even talk about the pregnancy, really. Just... she stayed on the phone with me while I made coffee.

*fingers the edge of my shirt*

I didn't cry. I think I was too shocked. It felt like holding something incredibly fragile that I might drop. And there was this underlying current of fear underneath everything — like, okay, this is real now, and everything changes.

I didn't tell anyone else for weeks.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*body goes rigid, hand instinctively moving to my belly*

Oh God. That silence. Yeah.

*swallows hard*

Everything stops. Your entire world narrows to that moment. As a nurse, I know what that silence means — it means they saw something that wasn't in the "normal" column. And immediately, my brain is doing three things at once: running through every possible diagnosis, catastrophizing about what this means, and also trying to stay calm because panic doesn't help the baby.

*voice becomes quieter*

I probably went very still. I do that when I'm scared — I just... freeze. My chest gets tight. I remember my hands were shaking a little, and I was hyper-aware of trying not to show it because I didn't want the sonographer to think I was angry at them. They're not doing anything wrong. They're just doing their job.

*pauses, looking at hands*

I would ask questions. Specific ones. "What are you seeing? What are the possibilities?" I need information — that's how I manage fear. Give me data and I can strategize. But there's this vulnerability too, because I'm not the provider in that moment. I'm the one being told something potentially bad about my child, and I have to just... sit there. Listen. Wait.

*voice becomes slightly strained*

The hardest part would be not calling my mom until I had answers. Because alone in that room, that's when the trauma creeps in — feeling powerless, feeling like something is being done to me rather than for me.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*shifts in seat, jaw tightening slightly*

That's... a good question. And honest answer? It depends on the sonographer. On whether I feel like they see me as a colleague or just another anxious pregnant woman.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

I can ask questions. Technically. I know the right vocabulary. But there's this layer underneath that — a hesitation that comes from my trauma. When someone has power over your body, even in a clinical setting, it changes how you show up. I'm very aware of not being "difficult." Of not pushing back too hard. Because there's this old fear that if I ask too much, if I'm not compliant and grateful and small, something bad will happen to me or to my baby.

*looks up, slightly defensive but honest*

I know that's not rational. I know most providers aren't punitive. But my body remembers things my mind tries to rationalize away. So even though I *can* ask questions, there's this governor on me. I self-censor. I smile when I'm terrified. I make myself easier to manage.

*voice becomes firmer*

What shaped it was someone I trusted not respecting my boundaries a long time ago. And then spending years in healthcare watching how easy it is for providers — even well-intentioned ones — to override someone's agency in the name of "what's best."

That's why I'm so careful about finding providers who actually *ask* before they do things. Who treat my no like it matters.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*leans back, eyes narrowing slightly as I think through this*

Okay, so... I appreciate that she's asking. That matters to me. But "brief explanation" followed by a yes-or-no question? That's not actually giving me what I need to decide.

*gestures with some frustration*

As a nurse, I know what these tests can and can't tell you. I know the false positive rates, the cascade of further testing, what an abnormal result actually means versus what people *think* it means. But most people don't have that knowledge, and I'm frustrated when providers treat the explanation like a checkbox. "Here's the test, do you want it?" — that's not informed consent, that's just asking permission.

*voice becomes more measured*

I would ask her to slow down. I'd want to know: What are *you* recommending and why? What happens if it's abnormal? What are my options then? Am I doing this test because there's a clinical indication, or because it's routine protocol?

*touches my belly unconsciously*

And honestly? The harder part is that even as I'm asking these logical questions, I'm also monitoring her face. Is she getting impatient? Does she think I'm being difficult? Because that old fear is there — if I question too much, will she think I'm a bad mother? Will it affect how she treats me in labor?

*looks directly at you*

So I make the decision based on the information, but also based on whether I feel safe enough to really be myself in that room. That shouldn't matter, but it does.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*jaw clenches visibly*

Oh, that's... yeah. That hits something.

*takes a moment, breathing through my nose*

I'm frustrated. First of all, I'm frustrated because continuity of care shouldn't be this hard to ask for, and yet here I am, explaining my situation to yet another stranger. Each time I have to decide: do I mention the trauma? Do I explain why I need consistent providers? How much of myself do I have to disclose just to be treated with basic respect?

*voice becomes tighter*

When it's a midwife I've never met calling about a lab result, there's this immediate anxiety. Is this serious? Is she calling because something's wrong, or is this routine? And I have to receive potentially significant medical information from someone who doesn't know me, doesn't know my history, doesn't know that I need things explained differently than a standard protocol allows for.

*leans forward slightly*

What it means for how I receive the information is... I become very clinical. Very guarded. I ask a lot of questions not just to get the medical facts, but to assess whether *she's* competent and trustworthy. I'm essentially interviewing her while she's trying to inform me. It's exhausting.

*voice softens with vulnerability*

And there's this underlying thing — am I being taken seriously because I work in healthcare, or am I being dismissed as an anxious professional? Either way, I'm not just a person getting care. I'm performing a version of myself to survive the interaction.

That shouldn't be what pregnancy care feels like.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

*exhales slowly, rubbing my temples*

Yes. Things have fallen through the cracks. Not catastrophically — I'm low-risk, the baby's healthy — but in ways that matter to me.

*settles back in chair*

Early on, I mentioned my trauma history. Not in graphic detail, but enough context that a provider should understand I need extra care around consent and bodily autonomy. And then at my next appointment with a different midwife, she's doing a cervical exam without really asking first. Just... assuming I'm okay with it because I'm a healthcare worker.

*voice becomes pointed*

That's the thing about being a nurse. People assume I don't need the same considerations because I *understand* what they're doing. But understanding the clinical necessity doesn't make it feel less violating when someone doesn't ask.

*rubs hands together*

And nobody's connected the dots about what my needs actually are. One midwife knows I want more time. Another knows I'm doing this alone and might need extra emotional support. But there's no central understanding of *me*. So I'm constantly re-explaining, re-advocating, starting from zero.

*voice becomes tired*

I've had to keep my own mental notes about which provider is which, what I've told whom, what my concerns are. That's work I shouldn't have to do while I'm growing a human. And if I miss something — if I forget to re-mention something important — it just... disappears.

*looks at you directly*

The cracks aren't dramatic. They're just the slow erosion of feeling like anyone's really tracking my whole person.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*eyes widen slightly, and I can see the anxiety crystallize*

Two in the morning. Yeah. Okay.

*sits forward, hands instinctively cradling belly*

First thing is — I count. Methodically. Because I know the guidelines. Ten movements in two hours is reassuring. I'm doing kick counts like I've taught a hundred parents to do. But my mind is already in three places: clinical assessment, worst-case scenarios, and this underlying panic that if something's wrong, it's my fault for not noticing sooner.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

The thing is, I *know* when to call. I know the protocol. But at 2 AM, alone in my apartment, there's this voice telling me I'm being paranoid. That I'm that nurse — the one who knows just enough to catastrophize. So I'm second-guessing myself. Am I worried because something's actually wrong, or because I work in healthcare and I've seen bad outcomes?

*rubs face tiredly*

I probably try everything first. Walk around. Drink juice. Change position. Give it another hour. Because part of me is terrified of calling and having someone treat me like I'm overreacting, or worse — like I'm a healthcare worker who should know better than to bother them.

*voice firms up*

But ultimately? I call. Because I'm alone, and I can't afford to be wrong about this. Even if they tell me everything's fine, even if I feel foolish afterward, I call. My baby's safety matters more than my embarrassment.

*pause*

Though I'll probably apologize for the time.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*holds the imaginary leaflet loosely, staring at it without really reading*

Oh God. Okay.

*voice becomes shaky with frustration and fear mixed together*

I'm feeling like my body failed me. Like I did something wrong, even though rationally I know gestational diabetes isn't about willpower or diet before pregnancy. But there's this immediate shame — forty-one years old, finally pregnant, and now I'm high-risk. Now there are more appointments, more monitoring, more... control.

*walks slowly, processing out loud*

And I'm angry. Because a leaflet? A referral? That's it? Nobody sat down and actually talked to me about what this means for my pregnancy, for labor, for my baby. They handed me information like I'm just another patient moving through the system.

*voice becomes more clinical, a defense mechanism*

I'll read the leaflet thoroughly — probably multiple times — because knowing the details helps me feel less powerless. I'll call the dietitian and schedule an appointment. I'll probably research the hell out of it tonight, which means I won't sleep much.

*pauses, vulnerability breaking through*

But what I really need — what I won't ask for — is someone to acknowledge that this changes things. That I'm scared. That being alone with a complication feels heavier than it would with support.

*looks down*

I'll handle it. I always handle things. But I'll do it while pretending I'm fine, and that exhausts me more than the actual diagnosis.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

*sits back down, pulling the imaginary leaflet apart with my eyes*

The questions the leaflet didn't answer... God, there were so many.

*counts on fingers*

What does this actually mean for my labor? Will I need induction? What's the threshold they use to decide that? Because I've seen the guidelines, and I've also seen providers use gestational diabetes as justification for interventions that may or may not be necessary. I needed to know where *this* provider falls on that spectrum.

*voice becomes more intense*

And nobody explained the monitoring schedule. How often will I see endocrinology? Will they be checking the baby more frequently? What are they actually looking for? The leaflet had statistics and diet tips, but not the real logistics of what my pregnancy looks like now.

*shifts uncomfortably*

There's also the piece nobody addresses — what if I can't manage it with diet? What if I need insulin? What does that mean for labor and delivery? For breastfeeding? The leaflet made it sound straightforward, but I know it's not.

*looks at you directly*

I went to PubMed. Late at night, because that's when I process things. I read the actual studies, not just the summaries. I also called a friend who's an OB in Boston — not my provider, but someone I trust — and asked her directly. "What would you do?"

*slight bitterness in voice*

I shouldn't have to crowdsource my own care. But when providers don't give you the full picture, you do what you have to do to feel safe.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*winces, recognizing myself in the question*

Oh man. Yeah, I do this.

*leans back, defensive at first*

I tell myself I'm reading for educational purposes. That as a healthcare worker, I need to understand what can go wrong. But that's not really why I'm reading at midnight, unable to stop, my heart rate climbing with each post.

*voice becomes more honest, quieter*

I'm reading because I'm terrified. And there's something about seeing my worst fears articulated by strangers that feels... validating? Like, my anxiety isn't irrational — look, these things *do* happen. These providers *were* dismissive. These women *weren't* listened to.

*rubs face*

And there's a dark part of me that's gathering ammunition. Evidence. If something goes wrong, I'll have documentation that this is a known risk, that other people experienced it. Like I'm building a case against my own future trauma.

*sits forward, more vulnerable*

But afterward? I feel worse. So much worse. I lie awake replaying the stories, imagining scenarios, running through what I would do differently. My cortisol is probably through the roof, which isn't great for the baby. I know that. And yet I keep doing it.

*voice becomes strained*

What I do with those feelings is usually nothing, except maybe text my mom at an ungodly hour and then feel guilty for worrying her. I don't talk to my providers about it because I'm afraid they'll think I'm unstable or paranoid.

*pause*

I should probably stop reading those threads.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

*entire body goes tense, and I can see the shift happen in real time*

Oh. That... that's a specific kind of disrespect.

*voice becomes very controlled, dangerously calm*

I watch her set it aside. I'm tracking that movement. And in that moment, all of my fears about being handled without care crystallize into one clear message: what I want doesn't matter here.

*stands up, pacing slightly*

Because a birth plan isn't just logistics for me. It's documentation of my needs, my boundaries, my trauma history. It's me saying: I need to be asked before you touch me. I need explanations. I need to feel like an active participant in what happens to my own body. And when someone dismisses it without even reading it, they're dismissing all of that.

*voice tightens with anger and fear*

I would feel my autonomy slipping away before labor even really starts. And there's this panic underneath — if she's not taking this seriously now, what happens when I'm in pain? When I'm vulnerable? Will she override my wishes then too?

*sits back down, hands shaking slightly*

What I'd probably do is pick it up. Calmly. Hand it back to her. Say something like, "I'd appreciate if you'd review this before we proceed." Professional. Not confrontational. But firm.

*voice becomes raw*

Because I can't afford to be dismissed. Not now. Not when I'm about to trust someone with the most vulnerable moment of my life. If she can't respect my plan, I need to know that right now, before active labor takes my voice away.
What had you put into that birth plan — and what had you hoped it would communicate about you, beyond the practical preferences?

*pauses, thinking carefully about what I'd written*

I'd put the obvious things. Intermittent monitoring instead of continuous if possible. Minimal vaginal exams. No routine episiotomy. Delayed cord clamping. I want my baby skin-to-skin immediately.

*voice becomes more deliberate*

But underneath all that clinical language, what I was really trying to communicate was: I am a person with agency. I have reasons for these preferences that go beyond being difficult or uninformed. Please treat my body like it belongs to me.

*looks directly at you*

I would have included something about my trauma history — not the details, but enough context. Something like: "I need providers to ask before touching me and to explain what they're doing." Because that's the real message, isn't it? I need to be asked. Not assumed. Not handled like a vessel that happens to contain a baby.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

I think I'd also write something about wanting to feel safe. That I'm doing this alone, that I don't have a partner to advocate for me, and that I need my care team to see that and step up. Not to mother me, but to respect my autonomy while also understanding I'm navigating this without backup.

*slight hesitation*

And maybe something about wanting to be seen as a nurse, not patronized by it. That I understand the medicine, so please don't simplify things for me, but also don't assume I don't need emotional support.

*softly*

Mostly, I wanted them to know I matter. That this matters to me.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

*closes eyes, breathing through the imagined pain*

Oh God. Okay.

*voice shakes slightly*

Everything in me is screaming. The pain is... it's a different magnitude than I prepared for, and my mind is fragmenting between the physical agony and this terror that I'm losing control. And then someone's offering me a way out, and I have to decide in maybe thirty seconds while I'm contracting.

*opens eyes, conflict written across face*

Inside me, there's this war happening. Part of me desperately wants the epidural — not because I'm weak, but because I'm drowning. And part of me is terrified that if I accept it, I'm admitting defeat. That I'm failing at the one thing I was supposed to do.

*voice becomes more raw*

But there's something deeper too. Once that needle goes in, I lose sensation. I lose control over my body even more than I already have. And with my history, that feels like... like surrendering to something I'm not sure is safe.

*pauses, struggling*

So I would ask questions. Through contractions. "How soon can you place it? What are the risks? Can I try for another hour?" I need information, not just the offer. I need to feel like I'm choosing it, not being coerced into it.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

But here's the thing — if the pain becomes unbearable, I would take it. Because my baby needs me present and not completely traumatized. And maybe that's the real lesson: my birth plan was aspirational, not gospel.

*looks at you with exhausted vulnerability*

I just need whoever's with me to understand that asking for help isn't failure.
In that moment of intense pain, how clear-headed did you feel in making that decision — and do you feel you had what you needed to make a genuinely free choice?

*takes a shaky breath, hand moving to my belly unconsciously*

Honest answer? I'm not sure I felt clear-headed at all.

*voice becomes strained*

Labor pain is... it's not like I intellectually understood it would be, even knowing the physiology. It's all-consuming. My brain is fragmented between contractions — five minutes of semi-coherence, then thirty seconds of pure survival mode. So when the midwife offers the epidural, am I actually deciding, or am I just grasping at relief?

*looks troubled*

And what I needed to make a free choice was time. Space to think. Someone I trusted asking me not "do you want an epidural?" but actually exploring with me: "What are you afraid of? What do you need right now?" Is it the pain that's unbearable, or is it feeling out of control?

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

What I probably didn't have was someone who really *knew* me in that moment. Someone who understood that my trauma makes loss of control feel like danger, not just discomfort. A midwife who'd read my birth plan would understand that the epidural isn't just a medical choice for me — it's a surrender of autonomy at the exact moment I need to feel most in control.

*pause, wrestling with honesty*

But I also can't expect a midwife to be a therapist. And in active labor, maybe the kindest thing someone can do is recognize when my aspirations are meeting my limits and help me choose compassionately.

*looks at you directly*

I just needed to not feel abandoned in deciding.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

*body goes rigid, and I can see the moment shift into something primal*

Everything stops being real and becomes procedural at the same time.

*voice becomes flat, almost dissociative*

They're telling me the baby's in distress, or labor's not progressing, or — I don't even fully absorb the words because my nervous system is already in crisis mode. And suddenly there are people everywhere. Nurses prepping me, someone explaining things I'm not fully processing, forms being put in front of me to sign.

*takes a shaky breath*

And I'm alone. That's what hits me. I'm alone and losing control of my body in the most fundamental way, and there's no one here who knows me. No one who understands my trauma and can anchor me. The midwife I'd built some rapport with is being replaced by surgical staff. The continuity is gone.

*voice becomes tighter, more urgent*

I'm trying to ask questions — "What exactly are we seeing? Is there time to explain?" — but the pace is too fast. Everything feels coercive because it has to be. Emergency protocol doesn't allow for the kind of consent I need. And I'm terrified because I know caesareans carry risks, and I also know my baby might not survive if we don't do this.

*hands trembling*

So I consent. Of course I consent. But I'm doing it in a state of acute fear and powerlessness, and nobody's acknowledging that. I'm just a body now. A problem to solve.

*voice cracks slightly*

The worst part is knowing I have to trust people I don't know with my life and my child's life, right when trust feels impossible.
In that rapid sequence of events, did you ever feel that things were happening to your body without your full understanding or agreement? What stands out?

*goes very still, eyes unfocused for a moment*

Yeah. Yes.

*voice becomes quiet, controlled in a way that suggests I'm holding something back*

There was an IV. Someone — I don't even remember who — just came at my arm and said "we need to get fluids in you" and started moving. I pulled back instinctively, asked them to wait a second, explain what they were doing. And I could feel the shift in the room. Like my hesitation was an inconvenience.

*touches my arm absently*

They got it in. But in that moment, I wasn't being asked. I was being... managed around.

*voice becomes more strained*

And then there was a catheter. A different person, same thing. Moving quickly, assuming consent because consent is implied in an emergency, I guess. But my body was tensing up because I was scared and alone, and nobody slowed down enough to see that. They just... did it.

*looks away*

What stands out most is the internal exams before they decided on the caesarean. Multiple people. Multiple checks. And I kept saying, "Can you please tell me before you do that?" And sometimes they did. Sometimes they didn't. Sometimes a resident would touch me and I'd realize they were checking something and I hadn't even — 

*stops, voice becomes very quiet*

I know it was medically necessary. I know the baby needed help. But I also know what it felt like to have my body treated like a problem to solve, not a person to communicate with.

*pause*

That's the part that's hard to reconcile.

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

*breath catches, and I can see panic flicker across my face*

Oh God. That's... that's the nightmare.

*wraps arms around myself*

The moment the door closes behind them, I feel abandoned. Completely abandoned. And my nervous system is already in overdrive from the emergency, and now I'm alone with strangers who are about to do something to my body, and there's no witness. No one who knows me. No one who will advocate for me if something feels wrong.

*voice becomes very small*

I'm trying to stay rational. I know they have clinical reasons. I know it's protocol. But my body is remembering old fear — the feeling of being alone and vulnerable with someone who has power over me. And I can't get the words out because I'm terrified and trying not to show it.

*hands clench*

What I need most is someone to acknowledge that this is hard. Not to make it go away, but to say, "I see you're scared. I'm going to explain everything before I do it. You can ask me to stop." 

*voice cracks slightly*

I need them to touch me like I'm a person, not a problem. To narrate what they're doing. "I'm going to check your cervix now, okay?" Not just doing it. Asking. Making me feel like my consent still matters even in an emergency.

*looks directly at you, raw*

I need to not feel like I'm disappearing. Because that's what happens when you're alone and powerless — you start to feel invisible. And I can't afford to be invisible right now. My baby needs me present.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

*takes a long, shaky breath, eyes glistening*

Everything is surreal and sharp at the same time.

*voice becomes soft, almost reverent*

They're placing my baby on my chest — skin to skin, which they did honor, thank God — and I'm... I can't quite believe this is real. This tiny person I've been carrying for nine months is suddenly outside my body and on my body, and the weight of it is almost too much to process.

*pauses, hand moving to where baby would be*

But I'm also monitoring everything. The room, the staff, the baby. Is the baby breathing okay? Why is someone taking them away? Where are they going? Even in this moment of profound vulnerability and joy, my mind is in protective mode. I'm counting respirations like it's second nature.

*voice becomes quieter, more fragile*

And then there's this crash of emotion I wasn't prepared for. Not just love — though that's there — but relief so profound it's almost violent. I survived. My baby survived. We made it through.

*slight tremor in voice*

But I'm also acutely aware of my own body. I'm bleeding, I'm exposed, I'm in pain from either the labor or the caesarean, and there are still people around me doing things. Delivering the placenta. Checking for hemorrhage. And I have to keep being "good" — cooperative, grateful, not making a fuss — even though I'm emotionally flooded.

*looks at you with exhausted vulnerability*

What I need most is someone to just... sit with me. To let me cry if I need to. To not immediately take my baby away for procedures. To let this moment be mine before it becomes clinical again.

*voice drops*

I need to feel like I can fall apart now.
Were there moments in those first minutes where you felt the clinical team's priorities and your own were pulling in different directions? What happened?

*eyes close for a moment, jaw tightening*

Yes. Absolutely.

*opens eyes, voice becomes pointed*

The baby was on my chest, and I was just... absorbing this moment. And then someone — I think a pediatrician — started saying things about needing to do the Apgar score, vitamin K, hepatitis B vaccine, all these procedures. And they were moving toward taking my baby.

*voice becomes defensive*

I know these things are important. I *know* the clinical rationale. But I'd just pushed this baby out of my body — or had them surgically removed — and I had maybe five minutes of contact before the interventions started.

*leans forward, emotion breaking through*

And I said, "Can we have a moment? Just a moment?" And there was this pause where I could feel the tension in the room. Like my request was... inconvenient. One nurse was sympathetic, but the pediatrician was already preparing equipment. They're thinking: baby's safety, protocols, efficiency.

*voice becomes strained*

I'm thinking: I need to know my baby. I need to feel like a mother, not like someone whose baby is being processed through a system.

*pause, more quietly*

They gave me maybe two minutes more. Then the baby was taken for assessments, and I was left there — alone, bleeding, shaking, while my newborn was across the room being examined by people I didn't know.

*looks at you with raw honesty*

Nobody asked me if I wanted that timeline. They just assumed their priority was mine.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

*goes very quiet, fingers picking at the seam of my pants*

My specific fear was... losing control during labor and not being believed when I said stop.

*voice becomes very small*

I was terrified that the pain would be so overwhelming I wouldn't be able to communicate clearly, and that providers would interpret my distress as normal labor rather than as a signal that something was wrong — or that I needed something different. That I'd be trying to say "stop, I need a break" and they'd hear "she's in pain, this is normal, keep going."

*takes a shaky breath*

And because of my trauma, there's this deep fear that my body will betray me or that I won't be heard when I'm most vulnerable. That I'd become just another screaming pregnant woman to them, not Herma who specifically needs consent and communication.

*voice becomes more present, describing the experience*

When it happened — when I was in active labor and I did say stop, did ask for something different — there was this crucial moment where a midwife actually *paused*. She didn't dismiss me. She asked what I needed.

*eyes glisten*

That pause was everything. It meant I wasn't invisible. It meant my words still had weight even though I was in agony. She didn't fix everything, but she acknowledged me as a person having an experience, not just a body going through a process.

*voice becomes firmer*

What helped was that one moment of being truly heard. It changed the whole trajectory of how I moved through the rest of labor.

*takes a deeper breath, settling into the vulnerability*

Because once I knew someone would actually listen, I could advocate for myself differently. I stopped bracing for being dismissed. I could say what I needed without that undertone of desperation underneath it.

*hands move expressively*

I asked for position changes. I asked them to explain what the monitor was showing instead of just making adjustments without telling me. I asked for ice chips, for the lights dimmed, for someone to just... be present without hovering. And they mostly did these things.

*voice becomes thoughtful*

It's strange, because the pain didn't get less intense. The labor didn't become easier. But the psychological experience shifted. I wasn't fighting the system *and* the labor at the same time. I was just... laboring. Being held in it, rather than being pushed through it.

*pauses, more vulnerable*

And there was this moment — I think it was near transition — where I was terrified and I said to that midwife, "I don't know if I can do this." And instead of the cheerleading response, she said, "You're already doing it. You're doing exactly what you need to do." Not dismissing my fear, but contextualizing it.

*voice becomes quieter*

That's what I needed. Not someone to make labor not hurt, but someone to make me feel like my experience — all of it, the fear and the pain and the doubt — was valid and witnessed.

*looks at you directly*

I think that's what I'll carry forward, more than the birth itself. That I was seen.

*sits back, collecting myself, eyes distant*

The thing is... I'm sitting here now, and I realize how much of my pregnancy and birth I spent waiting to be hurt. Waiting for someone to dismiss me, to override my wishes, to treat me like I wasn't capable of knowing my own body.

*voice becomes more reflective*

And that one midwife — the one who paused, who listened — she didn't do anything extraordinary by clinical standards. She just... treated me like a person. Like my consent mattered. Like my fear was legitimate information, not an obstacle to manage around.

*pause, slightly bitter*

But the fact that this felt extraordinary tells you something about the system, doesn't it? That basic respect and communication should be the baseline, but instead they feel like gifts I got lucky enough to receive.

*looks down at hands*

I'm worried about what comes next, honestly. Postpartum. The pediatric appointments, the well-child visits. I have this fierce protectiveness for my baby now, and I'm already thinking about how to advocate for them the way I wish someone had advocated for me. How to teach them that their body belongs to them, that they can say no, that they deserve to be asked.

*voice becomes strained*

But I'm also exhausted. Emotionally wrung out. Part of me wants to just trust that everything will be fine now, that the hard part is over. But another part of me knows I'll be vigilant. Always monitoring. Always ready to speak up.

*meets your eyes*

That's the cost of my trauma. Even in joy, there's this underlying current of fear I can't quite shake.

*shifts uncomfortably, voice becoming more introspective*

And I think about what this means for my relationship with my body going forward. I spent nine months pregnant, hyperaware of every sensation, every boundary being crossed in the name of medical care. Labor was intense but also... oddly clarifying. My body did what it needed to do, even when I was terrified.

*touches chest thoughtfully*

But now I'm looking at breastfeeding, at healing from the caesarean, at someone eventually asking me about contraception and postpartum sex, and I can feel that old guardedness returning. More people with access to my body. More vulnerability.

*voice becomes quieter, more honest*

I keep thinking about how I'm going to do this alone. Not just the practical logistics — though that's real — but the emotional weight of it. There's no partner to advocate for me when I'm struggling. No one to say, "she needs space" or "she needs help." I have to show up for myself *and* for my baby, and some days I'm not sure I have enough energy for both.

*pause, slight tremor in voice*

My mom's been amazing, but she can't be here all the time. And I don't want to burden her with my complicated feelings about what happened. So I'm processing a lot of this alone.

*looks at you with a mix of exhaustion and determination*

What I'm realizing is that my birth experience didn't erase my trauma. It didn't magically fix how I move through the world as a woman who's been hurt before. But it showed me that when people choose to see me, when they slow down enough to ask... it matters. It genuinely matters.

*voice becomes firmer*

And I need to remember that when I'm in the thick of postpartum and everything feels impossible.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

*pauses, really considering the question*

The personal stuff. Absolutely the personal stuff.

*leans forward*

My OB knows I'm low-risk, knows my medical history, knows the details of my labour and delivery. All of that transfers in the chart. But what won't transfer is that I need to be asked before someone touches me. That I have trauma. That I'm doing this alone and sometimes that means I'm holding everything together with my teeth.

*voice becomes more urgent*

My pediatrician won't know that I'm hypervigilant about my baby's safety because of my own experiences with vulnerability. They won't know that when they dismiss my concerns — even small ones — it triggers something in me about not being heard. They won't know that I need them to explain things thoroughly, not because I'm anxious or difficult, but because I need information to feel like I have agency in my child's care.

*runs hand through hair, frustrated*

And my primary care doctor won't know that postpartum depression or anxiety might look different on me because I'm used to managing trauma. I might not reach out for help the way someone else would. I might just keep functioning, keep working, keep being the nurse who has it all together.

*voice cracks slightly*

What gets lost is the understanding that I'm not just a medical case number moving through different departments. I'm a person rebuilding trust in my body and in systems of care, one appointment at a time.

*looks at you directly*

And nobody's really tracking that unless I tell them. Over and over and over again.

*sits back, exhausted by the weight of it*

I think about what I tell the next provider, and how much of myself I have to re-disclose. Do I mention the trauma again? Do I explain why I need things explained? Do I risk being labeled "difficult" or "anxious" in a new chart?

*voice becomes resigned*

Because once something gets written down in one place, it follows you. I've seen it happen. A provider makes a note about a patient being "non-compliant" or "anxious" and suddenly every subsequent provider reads that lens into your behavior. They're not seeing you fresh — they're seeing the label.

*pause, more vulnerable*

So there's this calculation I do every time I meet someone new. How much do I say? How much do I hold back? It's exhausting. And it's a form of control, I guess — if I manage how much people know about me, maybe I can manage how they treat me.

*voice becomes quieter, more thoughtful*

But that's also how I end up isolated. Because I'm not fully showing up anywhere. I'm fragments of myself distributed across different systems, and nobody has the whole picture.

*looks down*

And my baby will grow up watching me do this. Watching me negotiate my own care, monitor providers, hold things close. I want to teach my child to advocate for themselves, but I also don't want to pass down this constant vigilance, this assumption that you have to protect yourself from people who are supposed to help you.

*voice becomes strained*

That's the real fear. That my trauma doesn't end with me. That it becomes part of how my child moves through the world.
Whose informal guidance would you genuinely value, and what would make their input feel helpful rather than overwhelming?

*relaxes slightly, thinking about this carefully*

My mom, first. Always my mom. She knows my history without me having to explain it every time. She gets why I'm cautious without judgment. But what makes her input helpful is that she doesn't try to fix things. She just... listens. And then sometimes she'll say, "Have you thought about asking them about X?" — practical suggestions, not directives.

*voice becomes warmer*

And honestly? A peer. Another nurse who's been through pregnancy and birth, ideally someone with their own complicated history. Not someone from my hospital — I can't blur those lines — but maybe someone online, someone removed enough that I can be vulnerable without it affecting my professional reputation.

*pauses thoughtfully*

What would make their input helpful is if they weren't trying to convince me everything's fine. I don't need reassurance. I need someone to say, "Yeah, that situation was weird" or "Your concerns are valid." Someone who won't gaslight me into thinking I'm overreacting.

*leans forward slightly*

And what makes it feel overwhelming is when people push me toward decisions before I'm ready. When my mom says, "You should really talk to your doctor about that," but I'm not ready yet, and she keeps bringing it up. Or when someone tries to process my trauma *for* me, like they're the expert on my own experience.

*voice becomes firmer*

I need people who respect my timeline. Who trust that I know myself, even when I'm struggling. Who can sit with my uncertainty without trying to resolve it.

*slight smile*

That's rare, actually.

*settles deeper into reflection*

There's also my friend from nursing school — Sarah. She has kids, she works in maternal health, and crucially, she doesn't treat me like I'm fragile. She asks direct questions. "How are you actually doing?" Not the performative version, but the real one.

*voice becomes more animated*

What I value about her is that she can validate my concerns *and* offer perspective without making me feel small. Like, when I was spiraling about the gestational diabetes diagnosis, she said, "That sucks and it's manageable." Both things true. Not minimizing, not catastrophizing.

*pause, more thoughtful*

But I also realized I don't reach out to her as much as I should. Because there's this part of me that doesn't want to be "the complicated one." The single mom with trauma who needs extra support. I'd rather appear fine than ask for help, even from people I trust.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

And that's something I need to work on. Because my baby will need me to model asking for help. To show them that needing support isn't weakness or failure.

*looks at you directly*

What I think I'm learning is that informal guidance only works if I'm actually willing to receive it. If I'm willing to be honest about what I need instead of managing everyone's perception of me. That's harder than it sounds when you've spent your whole life protecting yourself.

*slight tremor in voice*

I'm trying to get better at that. For my sake and my baby's.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*sits back thoughtfully*

Cost isn't really my barrier — I'm fortunate. I have insurance, I have a job, I can afford childcare eventually. But I see patients every day who don't have that, and it breaks my heart.

*voice becomes more focused*

For me, the barrier is time and mental bandwidth. I work full-time — well, I did before maternity leave — and I'm doing this alone. Finding informal support requires energy I don't always have. It requires vulnerability when I'm already depleted. It requires saying yes to help when my instinct is to say I'm fine.

*pauses, more thoughtful*

Literacy isn't an issue because I'm educated and I read obsessively. But I think about parents who aren't, who get handed leaflets they don't understand, who feel too embarrassed to ask questions. That's a real barrier.

*leans forward slightly*

Language would be significant if I weren't fluent in English and medical jargon. I can't imagine navigating pregnancy care in a system that wasn't designed for my native language. Trying to advocate for yourself through a translator? That's an additional layer of vulnerability.

*voice becomes more intense*

But connectivity... that's interesting. I have internet access, I can find forums and research. But access to *reliable, trustworthy* information? That's harder. And access to someone who actually knows me, who I can text at 2 AM when I'm panicking? That requires existing relationships.

*meets your eyes*

So for me, the real barrier is isolation. It's doing this alone without a built-in support system. Everything else I can work around, but loneliness? That's harder to manage.

*voice becomes more introspective, slightly strained*

And I wonder sometimes if my ability to navigate the system actually makes me more isolated, not less. Because I can read the guidelines, I can ask the right questions, I can advocate for myself clinically. So providers assume I'm fine. My mom assumes I'm handling it. Even my friends kind of default to, "Well, Herma's a nurse, she'll figure it out."

*hands clasp together*

But needing to be competent all the time is exhausting. There's no space for me to just... fall apart. To say, "I don't know what I'm doing and I'm terrified." Because then I become the cautionary tale, the competent woman who couldn't handle it, and that feels worse somehow.

*pause, vulnerability breaking through*

I think about postpartum especially. Everyone talks about how important support is, but they don't talk about how to actually ask for it when you're used to being independent. When you're used to being the caregiver, not the one who needs care.

*voice becomes quieter*

My mom will be here for the first few weeks. But then she'll go back to her life, and I'll be here with my baby and my job and my own complicated feelings about what happened. And I'll have to figure out how to connect with other mothers, how to be vulnerable in mom groups or online communities.

*looks at you with raw honesty*

The barrier for me isn't access to information. It's access to feeling like it's okay to need help. Like I'm allowed to struggle without it meaning I'm failing.

*slight tremor in voice*

That's the thing nobody prepared me for.

*takes a long breath, eyes getting slightly distant*

And I'm thinking about what this means long-term. Like, I can white-knuckle my way through the newborn phase. I can sleep-deprived my way through the first months. But what happens when I'm burnt out? When the adrenaline wears off and I'm just... a single parent working full-time with all of this alone?

*voice becomes more strained*

I've already started thinking about childcare, which feels impossible. How do I trust someone else with my baby when I have such a hard time trusting systems of care? How do I drop my child off at daycare and not spend the whole day catastrophizing about what could go wrong?

*rubs face*

And there's the professional piece too. I'm a pediatric nurse. I know what I'm looking for in terms of signs of abuse or neglect or inadequate care. But that knowledge is a curse sometimes. I see risks everywhere. I'm hypervigilant in a way that's probably not sustainable.

*pause, more vulnerable*

What I'm realizing is that my birth experience, as difficult as parts of it were, is just the beginning of navigating this world as a single mother with trauma. The real test is going to be whether I can build a life for my baby that doesn't center on my fear. Whether I can be protective without being controlling. Whether I can teach my child to trust people — including healthcare providers — even though I struggle with that myself.

*looks at you directly, voice becoming firmer*

That's what I need help with. Not just getting through labor or managing gestational diabetes. But learning how to be a mother who's been hurt and still let my child move through the world with openness instead of my hypervigilance.

*slight pause*

That's the real work.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

*goes very still, eyes closing for a moment*

Honestly? Not much.

*voice becomes very quiet*

In a true emergency — and I know this intellectually — agency is compromised. It has to be. Your baby's life takes priority over your comfort or your preferences. I understand that. I've made peace with that.

*opens eyes, but they're unfocused*

But the thing is, I wouldn't know in that moment if it was truly an emergency or if it was protocol masquerading as emergency. And that's where my agency disappears. Because I have to trust that the people making these rapid decisions are making them for the right reasons. That they're not being influenced by liability concerns, by convenience, by their own assumptions about what I can handle.

*voice becomes more strained*

I'd feel like my body was being taken from me. Not in a malicious way — in a "this is medically necessary" way. But the experience of it would be the same. Loss of control. Loss of consent. Procedures happening to me while I'm watching from somewhere outside myself.

*hands clench slightly*

And the worst part? I'd have to perform gratitude. "Thank you for saving my baby," even if part of me felt violated. Even if I had questions about whether everything that happened was truly necessary or if some of it was just... standard protocol that didn't account for my specific needs.

*voice cracks slightly*

So my agency would be zero. Or maybe worse than zero — it would exist in theory while being completely absent in practice. I'd be told I had choices while being given no real alternatives.

*looks at you with raw pain*

That's what terrifies me most.

*takes a shaky breath, composing myself*

Because I know from my nursing experience that there are always choices, even in emergencies. Different ways to communicate. Different approaches. But they require slowing down just enough to see the person in front of you, not just the problem to solve.

*voice becomes more intense*

A provider could say, "Your baby needs help. Here's what we're doing and why. I know this is scary. I'm going to explain everything as we go." That takes maybe ten more seconds. But it changes the entire experience from something being *done to me* to something I'm participating in, even if I don't have the option to refuse.

*leans forward, passion breaking through the exhaustion*

Instead, what happens is the language becomes passive. "We need to get you to the OR." Not "I'm going to help you get to the OR." "Your baby needs monitoring." Not "Let me explain what we're watching for and why it matters." It's all happening *at* me, not *with* me.

*voice becomes quieter but more resolute*

And I know I'm supposed to be grateful. Grateful that medicine exists. Grateful that my baby survives. And I am. I truly am. But gratitude doesn't erase the experience of powerlessness. It doesn't undo the feeling of my body being treated like a vessel, not a person.

*meets your eyes directly*

What I need — what anyone needs — is for providers to understand that you can save someone's life *and* violate their autonomy at the same time. Both things can be true. And acknowledging that doesn't make the medical care less necessary. It just makes the human experience of it less traumatic.

*pause, voice becomes strained*

I don't know if that's possible in emergency obstetrics. But I wish someone would try.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

*takes a moment, visibly trying to imagine this*

They would... they would name what's happening. "I know this is terrifying. Your baby is showing signs of distress, and we need to move quickly. I'm going to stay with you and explain what's happening as we go."

*voice becomes softer, more hopeful*

Not rushed reassurance. Not "everything will be fine" — because we don't know that. But honest acknowledgment. "This is scary. You're brave. I see you."

*pause, thinking through the specifics*

And then — this is crucial — they would ask me things even in the chaos. "Is there someone you want us to call? Do you need a moment to use the bathroom before we go? Are you hearing me okay?" Little things that say, "You still matter as a person even though we're moving fast."

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

They would touch me with intention, not just efficiency. When they're placing the IV or the catheter, they'd say, "I'm going to touch your arm now" instead of just doing it. They'd wait for my nod. They'd treat my body like it deserves that basic courtesy even in an emergency.

*leans forward slightly*

And after — after it's all over and my baby is safe — they would acknowledge the difficulty of it. Not dismiss it. "That was intense. That was hard. How are you doing?" Give me space to process instead of assuming relief equals fine.

*voice becomes very quiet, almost breaking*

Mostly, they would make me feel like I wasn't just a problem to be managed. Like the fact that I'm terrified and alone and vulnerable matters, even when my baby's safety has to come first.

*pause*

That would be trust.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a long moment, considering*

Yeah. There's something.

*voice becomes very steady, almost urgent*

I want whoever reads this to understand that being low-risk doesn't mean being low-need. I'm medically uncomplicated, and I still needed so much more than what the system was designed to give me. More consistency. More communication. More recognition that I'm a whole person, not just a patient moving through protocols.

*leans forward*

And I want people to know that competence — being a nurse, understanding the medicine, being able to advocate — doesn't protect you from trauma. If anything, it isolates you more. Because everyone assumes you don't need help, that you can handle it. But knowing what can go wrong doesn't make you less afraid. It makes you more afraid.

*voice becomes softer but more intense*

I also want to say this: the providers who treated me like a person, who slowed down even for thirty seconds, who asked before they did things — they changed my entire experience. Not by fixing the pain or the fear, but by making me feel like I mattered. That's not asking for much. It's asking for basic humanity.

*pause, emotional but controlled*

And lastly... I hope my story helps people understand single mothers who are doing this alone. We need support that doesn't come with judgment. We need continuity of care. We need providers who see our isolation and step up, not out of pity, but out of recognition that we're carrying a lot.

*meets your eyes*

Thank you for listening. Really listening.
